acceptance_targets: [CH14_DLG_001] Pass if the revised beat at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_14.md:161-165 no longer uses the phrasing pattern 'Every village you touched' plus 'The contamination is still there,' while still stating plainly that Maren's prior 'clearings' widened contamination beyond standard catchment; fail if a reader comparing the chapter to the style bible can still spot the calibration line on first read.

[CH14_CRAFT_001] Pass if, by the end of the arrival section, a cold reader can answer both 'Why did the camp wait until dawn to assess him?' and 'What specific precaution protected the camp while he slept?'; fail if either answer still has to be inferred from mood alone.

[CH14_INCOMPLETE_ROSTER] Every character named in Ch15's death report was introduced with at least one identifying detail in Ch14. No character introduced in Ch14 disappears without accounting. No distinguishing trait is attributed to a character other than the one who displayed it.
[CH14_RESTAGED_CONFRONTATION] Ch14's opening confrontation does not replicate Ch7's expose-the-fraud structure. Thessa's skepticism, if present, takes a new form or serves a new narrative function.
[R-06] Chapter 14 contains at most one reference to Thessa's stained hands and zero uses of 'thirty years' in connection to Thessa's experience.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if the revised beat at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_14.md:161-165 no longer uses the phrasing pattern 'Every village you touched' plus 'The contamination is still there,' while still stating plainly that Maren's prior 'clearings' widened contamination beyond standard catchment; fail if a reader comparing the chapter to the style bible can still spot the calibration line on first read.
findings.problem: Thessa's accusation at the chapter's moral center reproduces calibration-only style-bible wording too closely. Reusing the phrasing pattern around 'Every village you touched' and 'The contamination is still there' in the defining condemnation reads as voice-blueprint leakage rather than original scene speech, which is especially damaging in a chapter-defining beat.
findings.rewrite_direction: Rewrite Thessa's rebuke at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_14.md:161-165 so it arises from this chapter's specific discovery: Maren has not merely failed to clear sites, he has broken pools open and smeared them past standard channels. Keep her blunt professional authority, but remove the borrowed phrasing pattern and build the line from the Crook's technical finding instead of the style-bible calibration line.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if, by the end of the arrival section, a cold reader can answer both 'Why did the camp wait until dawn to assess him?' and 'What specific precaution protected the camp while he slept?'; fail if either answer still has to be inferred from mood alone.
findings.problem: The camp reads Maren as a bounty-tagged contamination threat, Hale is explicitly judging whether he will kill them in their sleep, and Thessa says they will determine 'what [he] is' in the morning, yet the chapter lets him sleep by their fire without stating why the examination must wait or what containment measure makes that choice safe. Because the next day's judgment depends on these people reading as competent professionals, the overnight permissiveness feels convenient rather than procedural.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_14.md:49-79 and/or snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_14.md:93-105, add the concrete operational logic that makes the overnight delay credible: why dawn is the safer or more legible time to assess him, and what visible safeguard the camp uses in the meantime (for example a watch rotation, marked perimeter, enforced sleeping distance, or Thessa keeping him under direct observation). The fix needs to show procedure, not just suspicion.

findings.acceptance_test: Every character named in Ch15's death report was introduced with at least one identifying detail in Ch14. No character introduced in Ch14 disappears without accounting. No distinguishing trait is attributed to a character other than the one who displayed it.
findings.evidence: Ch14 names five sin-eaters at the Crook: Thessa, Kael, Petra, Errin, and Hale (introduced across lines 3540-3700). The continuity sheet specifies five sin-eaters. Ch15's death report (line 4057-4059) accounts for six people—survivors Thessa, Kael, Hale; dead Petra, Ruald, Denna—naming two characters (Ruald, Denna) who were never introduced in Ch14 and dropping Errin entirely.
findings.problem: The roster breaks between chapters. Ruald and Denna appear only as names in a casualty list, denying the reader any basis for registering their deaths as losses. Errin, who was introduced and given scene presence in Ch14, vanishes without explanation. The root-chewing detail established as Kael's distinguishing gesture (Ch14, line 3596) is attributed to dead Ruald in Ch15 (line 4059), transplanting a living character's trait onto a character the reader never met.
findings.rewrite_direction: Option A (preferred): Introduce all six sin-eaters in Ch14—Thessa, Kael, Petra, Errin, Ruald, and Denna—each with at least one distinguishing detail (gesture, voice tic, physical marker). Root-chewing belongs to whichever character dies with it. Errin must either survive into Ch15's accounting or die on-page. Option B: Reduce Ch15's death list to characters actually introduced in Ch14 (Petra and Errin die; Thessa, Kael, Hale survive). This requires rewriting Thessa's loss-reaction in Ch15-16 to match a smaller body count. Either option must reconcile with the continuity sheet's character count.

findings.acceptance_test: Ch14's opening confrontation does not replicate Ch7's expose-the-fraud structure. Thessa's skepticism, if present, takes a new form or serves a new narrative function.
findings.evidence: Ch14's Crook arrival recapitulates Ch7's dynamic: Thessa confronts Maren's fraudulence, demands credentials he lacks, and exposes him before peers. Ch7 (lines 1700-1750) runs the same pattern—Thessa's examination, Maren's deflection, public exposure of incompetence. Ch14 eventually reaches new ground (Maren's genuine contamination-clearing work), but the first half restages familiar conflict.
findings.problem: The reader experiences diminishing tension because the confrontation shape is recycled. Thessa-exposes-Maren is a one-time reveal; a second staging needs a fundamentally different power dynamic or new information to justify the structural repetition. The chapter does pivot, but the restaged portion occupies significant space before the pivot arrives.
findings.rewrite_direction: Compress or reframe the Ch14 confrontation opening so that Thessa's skepticism manifests differently than in Ch7. Options: (a) Thessa has already accepted Maren's presence and the tension is over what he'll be asked to do, not whether he belongs; (b) another sin-eater challenges Maren while Thessa unexpectedly defends him, inverting the Ch7 dynamic; (c) the confrontation is cut to a few lines of callback ('The same look she'd given him in the examination room') before moving to new territory. The goal is to avoid re-earning credibility that was already at stake in Ch7.

findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 14 contains at most one reference to Thessa's stained hands and zero uses of 'thirty years' in connection to Thessa's experience.
findings.evidence: Ch14 line 3572: 'a ceramic bowl in her stained hands'; line 3652: 'stained dark, edges worn smooth, a tool that had seen thirty years of genuine use'; line 3724: 'Thessa's stained hands'; line 3596: Kael's hands described via comparison to Thessa's staining. First established Ch7 lines 1637, 1657, 1673, 1777.
findings.problem: Thessa's stained hands are mentioned 4 times within Ch14 (lines 3572, 3596, 3652, 3724) plus 'thirty years' at line 3652. The trait was established 5 times in Ch7. Total: 9+ instances of this single physical descriptor.
findings.rewrite_direction: Keep ONE stained-hands reference in Ch14 (line 3572 on first reappearance is strongest). Cut staining from lines 3652 and 3724. At line 3596, describe Kael's hands independently. Cut 'thirty years' from line 3652.

non_negotiables: Maintain heading contract exactly (# Chapter N).
Elliptical clipping is not permitted in dialogue or narration.
Avoid transcript cadence in high-stakes dialogue.
Avoid stiff over-formality in dialogue; use contractions and colloquial rhythm where character-true.
Preserve productive dialogue roughness; do not clean living speech into balanced thesis statements.
Reduce procedural drift when it displaces embodied consequence.
Maintain character-specific voice signatures from style bible.
Honor aesthetic risk policy: sanitization is the primary craft risk — do not soften, euphemize, or retreat from the story's established register. When in doubt, err toward rawness.

revision_pass.focus: Prioritize structural and craft blockers first: causality, chapter architecture, stakes clarity, continuity logic, and all HIGH/CRITICAL defects.
revision_pass.label: HIGH/CRITICAL structural/craft fixes

aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This novel must commit fully to its own weirdness. A System-blind con man with a dead paladin and a dead assassin arguing in his skull is inherently absurd, and the novel should let that absurdity live alongside genuine grief, genuine violence, and genuine mystery without apologizing for the tonal range. The darkest moments and the funniest moments may occupy the same scene. When they do, let both registers operate simultaneously — do not sacrifice comedy for gravitas or gravitas for comedy. Risk means specificity: the specific sensation of a body doing things you did not tell it to do, the specific comedy of a dead paladin critiquing your hygiene while you're running for your life, the specific horror of carrying incomplete people whose memories have holes, the specific shame of learning that your eleven years of fraud may have killed people. When in doubt between too raw and too safe, choose raw. The novel's emotional range should be wider than any single genre label would suggest.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism in the narration of violence, bodily experience, moral compromise, or physical consequence is a draft-level failure. Maren is a con man who has seen hard things and done dirty work in poor villages for eleven years — his narration should not flinch where he would not flinch. The style bible's sensory bias toward tactile and proprioceptive detail means physical consequence must be rendered through the body: what it feels like to throw up, to kill someone, to have your muscles operate without your consent, to perform a ritual that requires you to open yourself to contamination. Summary and abstraction at the moment of consequence is always worse than presence.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the norm for all characters except Aldric, whose low contraction level is a deliberate character marker that should erode over the course of the novel. Uncontracted forms in other characters' speech are reserved for emphasis, deliberation, or ceremony. In a world of practical people doing unglamorous work, stiff diction is the exception.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: Alternate between hot and quiet chapters as a structural principle. No two consecutive chapters should open the same way — if one opens with action, the next should open with interiority or setting; if one opens with dialogue, the next should open with physical sensation or observation. Vary scene count between chapters: compressed single-scene chapters after multi-scene ones. Let quiet chapters stay quiet — do not insert action beats or revelations to maintain pace. The pacing engine is Maren's voice and the interplay between the three internal consciousnesses, not external plot pressure.
prose_style_profile.diction: Colloquial with earned elevation. Default register is smart but not literary — a con man's English, precise about people and vague about feelings. Elevation reserved for moments of genuine awe, grief, or the rare glimpse of something beautiful. Fantasy-register portentousness is a draft-level failure — no 'ancient darkness' or 'powers beyond comprehension.' The world is weird and dangerous and ordinary all at once, and the prose should match.
forbidden_drift_patterns: Sentence fragments imitating action-movie or thriller prose — subject-dropping and article-stripping for false urgency
Semicolon-heavy scholarly narration that distances the reader from embodied experience
Present-tense micro-shifts for intensity — the novel is past tense throughout, no exceptions
Filtering words ('he saw,' 'he felt,' 'he noticed,' 'he realized') when direct rendering is available
Fantasy-voice portentousness: 'ancient,' 'dark power,' 'something stirred,' 'a presence beyond reckoning'
Comic narration that winks at the reader or breaks the fourth wall instead of committing to the situation
Repeated metaphor templates — if a comparison structure appears twice in a chapter, the second instance is a tic
Emotional summary replacing scene presence: 'He was overwhelmed by grief' instead of rendering what grief looks and feels like in the body
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third person, locked to Maren throughout. Aldric and Vess are experienced as internal voices — they speak as italicized dialogue within Maren's consciousness, clearly distinguished from his own thoughts. Maren cannot access their thoughts directly; he hears what they say to him, not what they think. The narration inherits Maren's sensory limitations: the System interface is always mediated through Aldric or Vess's descriptions, never rendered directly until the single exception in Chapter 17.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Varied by emotional register. Default: medium-length sentences with periodic short declaratives for grounding. When Maren is performing (lying, charming, faking): smoother, more controlled rhythm with longer subordinate-clause chains. When Maren is honest or overwhelmed: choppier, more fragmented, more concrete. Fight scenes: visceral and slightly disoriented, reflecting Maren's System-blindness — sensation and proprioception without the visual data everyone else has. Quiet scenes: allow longer, more observational sentences. The rhythm should breathe differently in every chapter.
sensory_bias: tactile — Maren's primary sense channel; the physical world is how he navigates System-blindness
auditory — the hum of System activity, Aldric and Vess's voices, the specific sounds of quest-light and contamination
proprioceptive — Maren's body doing things he didn't choose; the internal sensation of carrying fragments; the physical cost of consumption
olfactory — contamination has smell; ritual has smell; the world stinks in specific ways that the System-sighted don't notice because they navigate by interface
